title: A Short Economic History of the United States
gist: In a Ones and Tooze podcast excerpt, historian Adam Tooze argues slavery contributed roughly the same share of US GDP (~12-14%) as its share of population, that America's socialist tradition was crushed by repression rather than absent (and is now resurging via the DSA), and that US immigration is 'distinctive but not unique' — a myth increasingly undercut by Germany's higher migrant share today.

themes.theme: Slavery as a central economic institution

insights.claim: In the 1800 US census, 17% of the population was counted as Black slaves; by 1860 that share fell to 12.6%, roughly matching the share of African Americans in the US population today.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: Grounds the scale of the slave economy in hard census data, useful context for any historical GDP-attribution or reparations-adjacent analysis.
insights.locator: I have a great debt to the quantitative side

insights.claim: Slaves made up roughly 30% of the US labor force in 1800, falling to about a fifth by 1860 due to white migration; within agriculture, where 90% of slave labor was employed, slaves were 37-40% of the agricultural labor force.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: Quantifies slave labor's disproportionate weight in the antebellum agricultural economy specifically, versus the broader labor force.
insights.locator: If you look at the labor force

insights.claim: Quantitative economic history estimates put slave labor's contribution to total US economic activity at roughly 12-14% of GDP in the antebellum era — comparable in scale to the manufacturing sector's share of the US economy today.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: A concrete quantitative anchor for how large the slave economy was relative to the whole, useful for framing historical-reparations or wealth-origin debates in economic terms.
insights.locator: And we can then do the math

insights.claim: The US Civil War was fundamentally a political-economy conflict over whether new western states would be admitted as slave-labor or free-labor states, not primarily an anti-racism crusade — only once that logjam broke could western railway buildout and territorial integration proceed.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: Reframes the Civil War as an economic-institutional conflict over labor-market extension, a framework for understanding how deeply slavery was embedded in 19th-century US growth strategy.
insights.locator: And in the historiography, this question is absolutely central

insights.claim: Enslaved people functioned as mortgageable productive capital — securitized financial products built on human collateral; Thomas Jefferson's Monticello estate was paid for in part via a mortgage raised on his slaves.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: A vivid illustration that slavery functioned as a securitized financial asset class, not merely a labor system — relevant to understanding early US capital markets.

insights.claim: Historiography on slavery's economic role has swung repeatedly: from an early liberal view of slavery as a backward institution overturned by Northern progress, to a mid-20th-century revisionist view that slavery was economically rational, to a more recent critical view (echoed by the 1619 Project) crediting American prosperity fundamentally to slavery's violence, to a newest line arguing slavery's efficiency was overstated and emancipation itself drove further growth.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: Maps the intellectual pendulum on slavery's economic role, useful for gauging which historiographical camp currently dominates policy and cultural discourse (e.g., reparations debates).
insights.locator: And this question of how historians have read slave institutions

insights.claim: Anti-Black racism's economic origins persist today most visibly in the mass criminalization and incarceration of African American men, which Tooze calls one of the 'besetting curses' of American society, structurally distinct from racism in Britain or Europe.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: Draws a direct causal line from slavery's economic logic to present-day incarceration disparities, framing a persistent social-cost variable in US demographic and labor-market analysis.
insights.quote: the reality of consequences of anti-Black racism running through American society all the way down to the 2020s, and most manifestly with regards to the criminalization and incarceration of African American men, it is shocking
insights.locator: It’s completely inescapable as a reality

insights.claim: Contrary to the common narrative that the US lacked a socialist tradition, 19th-century America hosted a powerful international socialist movement: Marx and Engels closely followed the US Civil War, and German 1848 revolutionaries (including Franz Sigel, commemorated by a Manhattan statue) fought for the Union army.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: Challenges the 'American exceptionalism from socialism' thesis with concrete historical evidence, relevant context for assessing the durability of today's US left-wing political resurgence.
insights.locator: AT: This is a really fascinating question

insights.claim: May Day (May 1), the global workers' holiday, commemorates the 1886 Haymarket massacre in Chicago, meaning the international labor movement chose a US labor struggle as its worldwide symbolic anchor.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: A specific, checkable historical fact undercutting the idea that the US was peripheral to global labor/socialist history.
insights.locator: And May Day, the workers’ holiday around the world

insights.claim: American socialism was not absent but actively crushed — via armed strikebreakers, an FBI created largely to monitor socialist threats, and the collapse of the post-WWI inflationary boom in 1920, which ended the era's largest surge in US labor militancy.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: Identifies state repression, not lack of demand, as the mechanism suppressing US socialism — a framework for assessing whether today's resurgence faces similar institutional pushback.
insights.locator: So part of the answer to the question of why socialism isn’t stronger

insights.claim: US socialism persisted through the 20th century via the CIO/UAW labor movement (Walter Reuther, a German émigré-socialist-family figure who built Soviet car plants in the 1930s), postwar social-liberal negotiations between the Kennedy/Johnson administrations and the New Left, and the Civil Rights Movement (Martin Luther King Jr. and Coretta Scott King, described as a genuinely radical socio-economic thinker).
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: Traces a continuous, if marginalized, socialist through-line from the 1930s to the civil rights era, contextualizing the ideological roots of today's US left.

insights.claim: The Democratic Socialists of America has roughly 50,000-60,000 members — comparable in scale to Germany's Die Linke — and DSA-linked, Mamdani-endorsed candidates have recently surged in US elections, explicitly invoking America's socialist historical legacy.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: A concrete membership benchmark for gauging the DSA's current political weight and its comparability to an established European left party, relevant to US municipal/state political-risk tracking.
insights.locator: Last time I looked, it had 50,000 or 60,000 members

insights.claim: Tooze argues the US self-narrative of being uniquely open to immigrants is a myth: America's migration pattern is 'distinctive but not unique,' resembling the broader white-settler-colonial pattern shared with Canada and Australia, and with comparably large-scale European migration to Argentina, Brazil, Uruguay, Chile, and Costa Rica.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: Undercuts American immigration exceptionalism with comparative data, useful for benchmarking US immigration policy against structurally similar 'white commonwealth' and Latin American peer economies.
insights.locator: I think maybe that would be the way I would put it

insights.claim: The US led the world in imposing restrictive nativist immigration laws in the 1920s, closing its migration window in a move that set a template followed by much of the rest of the world in the 1930s-40s.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: Frames today's renewed US immigration restrictionism as a historical repeat of a pattern the US itself pioneered globally in the 1920s.
insights.locator: And then America leads the world, really, in the 1920s

insights.claim: When the US reopened immigration in the 1960s, the new pattern was closer to a guest-worker model dominated by short-range migration from Mexico and later the rest of Latin America, followed by a further 20th-century wave of heavily Chinese, South Asian, and increasingly African migration.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: Characterizes the structural composition shift of US immigration since the 1960s, relevant to understanding today's demographic and labor-supply trends.
insights.locator: And then when America reopens in the 1960s

insights.claim: Germany currently has a larger share of first- and second-generation migrants in its population than the United States does, meaning the US is 'no longer in any way unique' in migrant-population share.
insights.speaker: Adam Tooze
insights.so_what: A specific comparative statistic that directly falsifies the 'uniquely immigrant nation' framing often used in US political and economic narratives.
insights.quote: Germany, I think, has a larger share of first- and second-generation migrants than the United States does
insights.locator: But in terms of share of people with migrant background
